<findings>

[observation] [info] Isolated files — no import edges between 8 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
[observation] [info] The archive.json file alongside prd.json provides a safety net for destructive PRD operations — this is a sound data integrity pattern worth keeping.
[observation] [info] Two execution log files (execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl) indicate log rotation is in use; verify the rotation boundary is atomic to prevent data loss during concurrent runs.
[observation] [info] Zero cohesion and zero coupling are expected for a runtime data directory — these JSON/JSONL files are written by rex tooling and consumed by AI agents, not imported by any module.
[pattern] [warning] The data-layer contract (never imported at runtime) is convention-only. Consider adding a lint rule or CI check that asserts no `.ts`/`.js` source file contains an import path resolving into `.rex/` to make the contract machine-enforceable rather than doc-only.
[anti-pattern] [warning] The two-phase pending-proposals.json → acknowledged-findings.json workflow has no crash-recovery sentinel. A tool run that terminates between the two writes leaves both files in an inconsistent state with no machine-detectable signal. This is a data-integrity gap that could cause silent PRD corruption on restart.
[suggestion] [warning] Add a top-level `schemaVersion` field to prd.json and config.json (and validate it on read). Without a version sentinel, a user upgrading rex with a breaking schema change will receive an opaque parse error rather than a clear migration prompt.
[suggestion] [info] Define an explicit retention policy for archive.json (e.g. keep last N entries or entries newer than T days). An unbounded archive file will grow silently and has no current pruning path other than manual deletion.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "Rex Runtime State" (rex-runtime-state) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds

</findings>

<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 8 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- These are data files (JSON, JSONL, Markdown), not source code — the zone has no imports or coupling by design, functioning as a pure data layer for the rex tool
- The presence of both execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl suggests a rotation or archival mechanism; confirm the rotation logic handles edge cases like partial writes
- acknowledged-findings.json and pending-proposals.json represent a two-phase commit pattern for PRD proposals; ensure these are kept in sync and never left in an inconsistent state across tool runs
- Zero cohesion and zero coupling are expected for a runtime data directory — these JSON/JSONL files are written by rex tooling and consumed by AI agents, not imported by any module.
- Two execution log files (execution-log.jsonl and execution-log.1.jsonl) indicate log rotation is in use; verify the rotation boundary is atomic to prevent data loss during concurrent runs.
- The archive.json file alongside prd.json provides a safety net for destructive PRD operations — this is a sound data integrity pattern worth keeping.
- The zone functions as a one-way data sink: rex CLI commands write to it, AI agents and downstream tooling read from it, but no module ever `import`s from it. This write-only/read-by-convention contract is architecturally sound but entirely unenforced — nothing prevents a future module from `require`ing a JSON file here and creating a hidden runtime dependency.
- The data-layer contract (never imported at runtime) is convention-only. Consider adding a lint rule or CI check that asserts no `.ts`/`.js` source file contains an import path resolving into `.rex/` to make the contract machine-enforceable rather than doc-only.
- The two-phase commit pattern (pending-proposals.json → acknowledged-findings.json) has no observable recovery artifact: if a tool run crashes between write phases, the inconsistency is silent and discoverable only by manual inspection. A lock file or in-progress marker would make partial-write state machine-detectable.
- The two-phase pending-proposals.json → acknowledged-findings.json workflow has no crash-recovery sentinel. A tool run that terminates between the two writes leaves both files in an inconsistent state with no machine-detectable signal. This is a data-integrity gap that could cause silent PRD corruption on restart.
- The JSON runtime files (prd.json, config.json, archive.json) have no observable schema version field in their filenames or documented envelope format — format migrations cannot be detected or gated by tooling, creating a silent forward-compatibility hazard when the rex data model evolves
- archive.json has no documented size bound or eviction policy — over successive PRD cycles it will grow monotonically, with no mechanism to prune superseded entries short of manual deletion
- Add a top-level `schemaVersion` field to prd.json and config.json (and validate it on read). Without a version sentinel, a user upgrading rex with a breaking schema change will receive an opaque parse error rather than a clear migration prompt.
- Define an explicit retention policy for archive.json (e.g. keep last N entries or entries newer than T days). An unbounded archive file will grow silently and has no current pruning path other than manual deletion.

</insights>
